title: Eccles cakes
subtitle: Flaky pastry rounds with a spiced currant filling
excerpt: Eccles cakes are small, round pastries made from puff pastry filled with currants, dark brown sugar, and mixed spice. The pastry is cut into circles, folded over the filling, then pressed flat and scored on top so the filling is just visible. They bake until blistered and lacquered.
sourceNotes: Eccles cakes are named after Eccles in Lancashire, England, and are documented there from at least the eighteenth century. The combination of currants, sugar, and spice inside a flaky pastry round appears in northern English pastry traditions; early records describe them as a fair food and bakehouse staple. The specific form using puff pastry and a currant-and-spice filling is documented in British domestic baking literature from the nineteenth century, including in Florence White, Good Things in England (1932).

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked and cooled. Reheat from frozen in a 200°C oven for 8 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best eaten on the day, still warm or at room temperature. Keep in a tin for up to 2 days.

content.text: The shaping is the whole technique: place the filling in the centre, bring the pastry edges up and over it, pinch firmly to seal, then turn seam-side down and press the ball flat. The filling redistributes to fill the whole disc. Cut three slashes across the top so the currants are just visible and steam can escape; without slashes the pastry puffs unevenly. The egg white glaze and caster sugar give the characteristic lacquered, crystallised surface.

Heat the oven to 220°C fan. Line a baking tray with baking paper. Mix the currants, dark brown sugar, melted butter, mixed spice, nutmeg, and lemon zest in a bowl. Unroll the cold puff pastry on a lightly floured surface and cut into 10 cm rounds. Place a heaped teaspoon of filling in the centre of each round. Bring the edges up and over the filling, pinch firmly at the top to seal completely, then turn seam-side down. Press each ball flat with the palm of your hand to make a disc about 8 cm across. Cut three parallel slashes across the top.

Place on the prepared tray. Brush the tops generously with egg white and sprinkle with caster sugar. Bake for 18 to 20 minutes, until deep golden and blistered. Cool on a rack for 10 minutes before eating; the filling is very hot straight from the oven.
